Risks and potential concerns:

There are several risks that an investor should be aware of prior to investing in the strategy covered above.

  • Market risk: Barring a couple of investments, nearly all of the investments in this portfolio are invested in equity stocks. So, if the broader market were to correct or take a deep dive for any unforeseen reason, this portfolio would also lose value, though likely to a lesser extent due to the hedging portfolio.
  • Limited downside protection: Only one of the three buckets (which only represents slightly over 1/3rd of the capital) has an in-built hedging mechanism. The other two buckets, representing nearly 2/3rd of the capital, have no downside protection or hedging. In case of a deep correction, it will likely lose value as much as the S&P500, sometimes more.
  • Tax implications from Bucket-3: The hedging bucket requires monthly trading (from 0 to 4 trades). If this bucket were implemented inside a taxable account, the investor would have to track the gains and losses, and there may be significant short-term tax gains and tax liability. However, there would be no such issues if this were implemented inside a tax-deferred account.
  • Ongoing Rotation effort: There is going to be an ongoing monthly effort to maintain rotation on bucket-3. This requires discipline and a certain level of conviction to stay on the course even during times when this bucket may be underperforming.

Concluding Thoughts:

The market has been on a tear in the last couple of years and has been making fresh all-time highs on a regular basis. Even then, there are a lot of uncertainties and fear of a bubble and a recession in 2026. This is nothing new, though, as we can never be certain of the future direction of the market.

Our long-term approach focuses on what we invest in rather than when to invest. Keeping in mind the current market conditions, we have presented an investment strategy that we like to call a near-perfect portfolio strategy, with three diverse buckets, each serving a specific set of goals. The strategy presented in this article aims for moderate income, low drawdowns, and market-beating growth.

data.title: 5 Blue-Chip Dividend Compounders I'm Buying On Discount
data.description:
Who doesn't love a good "buy the dip" opportunity?

Especially when the market is spooked about risks centered on a part of the economy to which your humble dividend growth portfolio has little exposure. (Maybe that's just me.)

Every week (except for when I get distracted by ETF analysis like I did last week), I give my thoughts on the state of the economy, markets, and my dividend growth buy list. This week, it's back to normal.

Here's the agenda:

  • Painting a picture of the current state of the US economy to keep in the back of our minds.
  • How high market valuation and potential overinvestment into AI infrastructure might be creating a perfect storm that could result in a lost decade for stocks.
  • Some comments on my dividend growth buy list, which includes a few new names this week.

Onward!

The Big Picture

A little over a month ago, I wrote an article that included seven major points collectively painting a picture of our current economic environment. Without reproducing all seven points in their entirety, here they are in brief:

  1. US population growth is low and still falling, as a below-replacement fertility rate combines with low net immigration. (Headwind to GDP growth.)
  2. Data shows that Americans (primarily businesses, for now) are paying for tariffs. (Another headwind to GDP growth.)
  3. The housing market is frozen. (Yet another headwind to GDP growth.)
  4. The labor market is frozen, which is both a cause and a consequence of a weak economy outside of the AI ecosystem.
  5. The AI ecosystem is the biggest driver of economic growth by a wide margin, and there are no signs that AI capex are slowing down anytime soon.
  6. Consumer inflation is likely to experience a temporary, slight bounce over the next year as tariffs are passed through to consumers, but this will be offset by disinflation elsewhere.
  7. The Fed Funds Rate is likely to be lower 6 months and a year from now, providing a boost to rate-sensitive parts of the economy.

See the above-linked article for further discussion of these points.

In what follows, I want to dive in a little deeper into just Point #5 about AI capex showing no signs of slowing down.

What about the AI overinvestment bubble that everyone is talking about?

The prevailing line of thinking seems to be AI investment is or will be in a bubble but that this bubble is nowhere near popping.

That is, this perspective says that we are in the midst of overinvestment into AI infrastructure and overvaluation of AI assets but that none of the traditional catalysts for a popping of the bubble (e.g. the inability of some bubble players to service or refinance debt or the mass realization of unsustainable price increases) are visible yet.

Let's dig into that a little more.

The AI Bubble Risks A Lost Decade For Stocks

Stock price appreciation for the public companies within the AI ecosystem have been phenomenal over the past 3-5 years.

The Magnificent 7, which have irons in virtually all fires of growth in the US economy, have accounted for a stunningly high degree of stock market returns over the last half-decade:

To be fair, this chart is a month or so out of date at this point. It doesn't show the most recent selloff in AI-tech names.

Here's a more recent and detailed chart, comparing the returns of the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) against the broader AI ecosystem (CHAT), the Nasdaq 100 index (QQQ), and the S&P 500 (SPY):

The recent selloff in AI-tech, which commands more than enough weight to drag down entire indexes, has been significant.

As of midday on Thursday, November 20th, CHAT is down ~15% from its high, while the Mag 7 are down about 8% and the Nasdaq is down about 7%.

Naturally, investors are going to be asking if this is a buyable dip or the beginning of a bubble popping event. I can't answer that. But I do think it's instructive to sketch out why the market is spooked right now.

It starts with the incredible performance of AI-tech stocks in recent years, driven partly by earnings growth and partly by multiple expansion.

Next, it's necessary to understand the rising risk around the fundamental source of funding for AI investment.

Over the past few years, the vast majority of AI capex funding originated from the massive cash flows of the hyperscalers.

Over half of these five giants' considerable cash flows going into capex is an incredible fact. An extraordinary amount of money is being spent to built out artificial intelligence infrastructure, from data centers to high-end semiconductors to batteries to power generation plants and electrical equipment.

In addition to the hyperscalers' cash flows, another source of funding has been circular financing deals within the AI ecosystem.

In essence, much of the hyperscalers' cash flows that are flowing into the semiconductor makers are then getting reinvested back into AI software developers, who are paying the hyperscalers for cloud storage, which then results in more demand for semiconductors.

In essence, cash from the hyperscalers' balance sheets seems to have been transferred over to the major semiconductor producers' balance sheets.

This circular flow of spending within the AI ecosystem is pushing net income higher, and it is certainly fueling massive growth in free cash flow at the major semiconductor companies, but it is notable that free cash flow at the "Core Four" big tech companies over the last few years has been flat to down.

In other words, the hyperscalers' free cash flows, the most fundamental source of funding for AI capex, are not growing.

And the free cash flows of the AI software developers are nonexistent. OpenAI, Perplexity, and their ilk are in deep cash-burning mode and are expected to stay that way for at least the next three years.

So where is the funding for the trillions of dollars of planned AI capex in the next five years going to come from?

Research from JP Morgan indicates that of the $5 trillion in AI capex over the next five years, about $1.5 trillion will come from organic company cash flows. About $1 trillion will come equity issuance and creative financing techniques. Another $1.5 trillion will likely come from high-grade bond issuance.

For context, $1.5 trillion represents about 13% of the total market cap of the five big hyperscalers.

And as of April 30th, Fitch estimates that the total size of the non-financial investment grade bond market sits at $6.0 trillion. Thus, if the JP Morgan calculations above are correct, then data center buildout over the next five years will require bond issuance equivalent to 25% of total current non-financial IG bonds outstanding.

And that still leaves a funding gap of almost $1.5 trillion!

The knowledge that AI investment plans will require far more than what can be funded from the hyperscalers' cash flows alone is surely driving bubble fears right now.

And an AI bubble is by far the biggest risk most fund managers see in the market today.

And if an AI bubble is already a concern, then it will only become more of a concern over time as more and more planned spending becomes actual spending.

Current spending on AI infrastructure is significant, but it is merely the tip of the iceberg compared to the vastly larger planned spending under the surface.

One estimate says that AI investment in 2025 accounts for about 1.3% of nominal GDP, but that percentage should grow to 5.5% by 2030 if all planned spending is realized.

That would make the AI infrastructure buildout the largest investment boom in US history, bigger even than the railroad bubble of the 1870s-1880s.

It is perhaps unsurprising, then, that for the first time in the history of the Global Fund Manager Survey, more respondents (20% on net) think companies are overinvesting right now.

At no point in the last two decades has a consensus of fund managers said that companies are overinvesting. And now, the share is not just slightly positive. It's a net positive of 20%!

So, to review:

  • AI-related stocks account for the vast majority of stock market gains in recent years.
  • These gains are driven partly by earnings growth and partly by multiple expansion, but much of the earnings growth is fueled by a circular flow of spending within the AI ecosystem.
  • Hyperscaler cash flows have been the primary source of funding for AI infrastructure so far, but future capex will have to be funded mostly by other sources, including debt issuance.
  • Most investors think there is overinvestment in AI infrastructure right now, but it will take years before we know whether profitable uses of AI will create demand commensurate to future data center capacity.

It is possible that all future data center capacity will be quickly absorbed by companies with profitable uses of AI.

Or maybe there will be demand, but uses of AI will continue to be persistently unprofitable (as they are now). That would likely lead to significant margin compression for most if not all of the AI ecosystem.

Or maybe these future data centers will deliver to an increasingly oversupplied market in which there isn't enough demand, whether profitable or unprofitable.

Your humble financial analyst has no idea which one of these three scenarios will play out, but the first two scenarios seem plausible enough to make me highly cautious.

When the S&P 500 (SPY) reaches a ~23x forward P/E multiple, as it has now, forward returns have historically been poor, on average.

There have been good forward 1-year returns at the current index valuation, although there have also been as many or more instances of negative returns from the current valuation.

But when it comes to 5-year forward returns, there isn't a single historical instance of positive returns.

As such, the stock market appears to be at risk of a dreaded "lost decade," or at least a lost half-decade.

Maybe this time is different, but this data should at the very least give investors pause.

If the current tech investment cycle (and potential bubble) plays out similar to how the Dot Com bubble played out, then I think I will ultimately be very happy with my portfolio concentration in REITs and moderate-yielding dividend growth stocks.

Back in the late-1990s and into 2000, after multiple years of massive outperformance over real estate stocks (VGSIX) and other dividend-paying stocks (VEIRX), the Dot Com bubble burst, and the tech stocks in the Nasdaq index deflated while REITs and dividend stocks hugely outperformed.

I don't know for certain that history will repeat or "rhyme," of course, but I do believe there is far less risk in reasonably valued REITs and dividend stocks than there is in AI-related tech.

The current market selloff provides a reminder of that.

My Dividend Compounder Buy List

Speaking of the recent selloff, there are plenty of compelling buying opportunities for dividend growth investors in the market right now. The five names on my buy list simply represent the positions I'm trying to build and grow in my portfolio right now.

There are other compelling stocks like VICI Properties (VICI), the premier casino REIT trading at 12x AFFO and a ~6.3% dividend yield, that look very buyable but aren't a priority for me right now. In VICI's case, it is already one of my largest holdings and still a little above my cost basis.

These five are stocks (and one ETF) that I am accumulating in order to grow into larger positions in my buy-and-hold dividend growth portfolio.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)

American Tower (AMT)

 3.8% Mid-Single-Digit
Ares Management (ARES) ~3.5% High-Single-Digit to Double-Digits
Blackstone (BX) ~3.5% High-Single-Digit to Double-Digits
Public Storage (PSA) 4.5% Mid-Single-Digit
VictoryShares Lg Cap High Div Vol.-Weighted ETF (CDL) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit

American Tower

AMT is one of the largest owner-operators of telecommunications infrastructure in the world. It also now boasts the strongest balance sheet and lowest leverage among its tower peers.

I believe AMT's price weakness recently has come because of delays in some carrier leasing plans, but the long-term outlook for mobile data usage and the necessary investment into network infrastructure still look solid.

AMT also owns CoreSite Realty, a colocation data center subsidiary that should be a secondary beneficiary of the long-term growth of AI.

Ares Management

ARES has been on my watchlist for a long time, and I only recently opened a position in it. It is an alternative asset manager specializing in private credit. It has almost $600 billion in assets under management, and 2/3rds of that is in various credit strategies.

ARES is the industry leader when it comes to private credit. I have owned its business development company, Ares Capital (ARCC), for a long time and have observed their commitment to credit quality and thorough underwriting standards.

The stock has sold off along with other names associated with private credit, largely because of a few bankruptcies like First Brands to which ARES had no exposure. As of Q3 2025, only 1.0% of ARCC's loans by fair value were on non-accrual status (or 1.8% by cost).

And with the secular shift of commercial & industrial loans from banks to private credit still ongoing, I think ARES has a long growth runway still ahead of it.

Blackstone

What ARES is to private credit, BX is to commercial real estate. BX is a leading alternative asset manager with over $1.2 trillion in AUM. Over $900 billion of that is fee-earning, and about $500 billion of it is perpetual capital.

BX is known for its private REIT, BREIT, which is one of the largest and most successful private commercial real estate funds ever. But the asset manager has successfully grown not only its real estate equity and credit strategies but also other strategies like private equity and credit.

When it comes to asset managers, I like to stick to the biggest like ARES and BX, because I believe their scale and reputational advantages are huge. They are also able to attract the best talent, and they generally have access to the best and highest quality investment opportunities.

Public Storage

As I've written about in recent weekly updates, the whole self-storage space is suffering a slow occupancy decline in the midst of ultra-low job growth and housing turnover, two of the primary catalysts for storage unit demand. The marriage and birth rates are also quite low, which further diminishes demand for self-storage.

The good news for PSA is that its scale, efficiency, balance sheet strength, and financial conservatism put the REIT in a fantastic position to take advantage of the next self-storage bull market, whenever it comes around. Construction starts for self-storage properties also continues to decline as construction costs continue to climb (not helped by steel and aluminum tariffs).

Management is making up for organic softness through portfolio expansion--opportunistically acquiring storage properties in attractive markets.

CDL ETF

CDL has a surprisingly solid track record as a dividend growth ETF. Not only does it yield over 3%, but it has also averaged over 7% annual dividend growth over the last decade.

Moreover, slightly over a quarter of CDL's portfolio is in utility stocks.

I love this exposure to electric utilities, because I believe that the massive buildout of data centers in the coming years (whether profitable for the AI software developers or not) will result in a secular growth cycle for utilities.

Also, the low exposure to technology and consumer cyclicals strikes me as defensive in the face of a potential re-run of the Dot Com bubble.

data.title: PDI: This 15% Yield Is Finally A Buy
data.description:
Article Thesis

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) is a mutual fund that has always offered a relatively high income yield, but following a considerable share price pullback over the last couple of weeks, its dividend yield has risen to an even higher level -- it's 15% now. At the same time, PDI's share price pullback has made its valuation decline -- PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund now trades at a below-average premium to net asset value, which to me suggests that right now is a better-than-average time to buy this income investment for those who are interested in holding PDI's fixed-income assets.

Past Coverage

I have written about PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in May, when I cautioned against buying PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund at the time, giving it a neutral rating only. I argued that the net asset value premium was too high back then, which meant investors were at risk of overpaying. Since then, shares have pulled back, PDI's dividend yield has risen, and its net asset value premium has declined a lot -- which changes the thesis, as I will lay out in this article.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund: The Macro Picture

There are investments where company-specific items play a huge role, and there are investments where the macro picture plays a huge role -- such as the commodity price outlook or interest rate movements. As a fixed-income investment fund, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund belongs to the latter category: its performance and outlook are largely driven by factors that aren't under management's control, such as market interest rate movements, Federal Reserve policy, and so on.

With its multi-sector approach, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has solid diversification across different fixed-income assets, including mortgages, at around one-quarter of PDI's assets; high-yield credit, at around one-quarter, too; and government bonds, municipal bonds, etc. While not all of these assets are especially low-risk, such as high-yield credit, risks aren't ultra-high, either, and defaults have not been a major issue in the recent past. Still, the value of fixed-income assets can move substantially even without defaults, due to the connection between market interest rates and fixed-income prices: when market interest rates move higher, fixed-income asset prices, e.g., of treasury bonds, move lower. The reverse is true as well -- if market interest rates move lower, the market price of fixed-income assets moves higher. This impact is larger for assets with long durations, such as 30-year bonds, while the impact is smaller and even negligible for short-duration assets. PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has a weighted average duration of a little more than 3 years across all of its investments, which means that there is a meaningful price/value impact from interest rate movements, but it's not as large as what we see in very long-dated assets, such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

Over the last half year or so, market interest rates moved down to some degree, with 10-year treasury rates declining from around 4.5% to around 4.1%. This was driven by several contributing factors:

First, many investors realized that inflation worries were a bit overblown -- despite the tariffs that were announced in 2025, we are not seeing inflation spikes on a broad basis -- core CPI inflation is running at around 3% right now, more or less in line with 2024 and 2023. With inflation not spiking, despite some earlier fears that that would happen, interest rates have pulled back to some degree.

Second, we have seen a Federal Reserve interest rate reduction, and we have gotten some guidance about more cuts being likely over the next year. While Federal Reserve rate cuts do not impact longer-term rates immediately, they can result in declining longer-term rates down the road. Together with some uncertainties about economic growth, this has brought down interest rates over the last couple of months.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund has benefited from that dynamic via a growing net asset value: Its NAV per share stood at $16.67 half a year ago and stands at $16.85 as of November 20. While this is arguably not a huge increase, at around 1%, it is an increase nevertheless -- PDI has experienced a declining net asset value throughout most years in the past, so the recent firming of its per-share net asset value is good news. The declines in the past were partially driven by the fact that PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund tends to pay out more via dividends than it generates via interest income, which means that dividends were partially paid from its NAV. While this hasn't changed per se -- after all, its weighted interest yield isn't 15%, while its dividend yield is 15% -- the interest rate tailwinds we saw over the last couple of months have helped it keep its NAV from declining.

If we see further interest rate cuts in the next couple of quarters, possibly as early as December, market interest rates could continue to trend down to some degree. This would be beneficial for PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's NAV, and we may see some further NAV increases in that scenario.

The downside, however, to declining interest rates, is that PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund gets a lower average interest yield when it deploys new money. Since some of its investments mature every year, it has to redeploy cash regularly -- if interest rates decline, the yield on these new investments will be lower, all else equal. Over time, this could result in some pressure on PDI's interest income, although that impact will only materialize over time.

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund: Dividends And Valuation

PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund offers a very high dividend yield right now, following a share price decline of around 10% over the last year, which made its already high yield even higher. At current prices, investors get a ~15% annual yield from PDI. Of course, a dividend yield this high brings up questions about the reliability of the dividend. PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's dividend track record looks pretty solid, as there has not been any dividend cut so far. PDI hasn't increased its dividend in recent years, with the last dividend increase being from 2015, but with a starting yield this high, dividend increases aren't needed for a strong income stream. Investors can reinvest some dividends if they want to grow their income stream over time, e.g., if they don't need all of the dividend proceeds today.

I think it's worth noting that PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund's dividend is partially paid from its net asset value, due to the aforementioned fact that the interest yield on its investments isn't high enough to cover the dividend throughout the cycle. Over longer time frames, PDI's net asset value has thus trended down to some degree. I wouldn't be surprised if the same happens in the future, too, but thanks to the very high dividend yield, some NAV declines, as long as they are benign, aren't too much of a problem.

The best way, I believe, to evaluate PDI's valuation is its net asset value multiple. At current prices, PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund trades at a 3% to 4% premium versus its net asset value. In the past, this premium has oftentimes been in the 10% to 20% range, thus the net asset value premium is rather small today, historically speaking. This suggests a better-than-average current valuation and that right now is a better-than-average time to buy.

When I last covered PDI in spring, the net asset value premium was in the double digits, which was too high for my taste, but today, PDI looks more attractive. While long-term price appreciation seems unlikely to me, PDI could still be a solid investment today for those with a clear income focus, as its valuation is at a below-average level, while its dividend yield is pretty high, and since PDI could benefit from further interest rate declines in the coming quarters.

data.title: Belpointe PREP: Still An Interesting Speculative Growth Play In The REIT Sector Trading At 0.5x NAV
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I see Belpointe PREP, LLC (OZ) as a different real estate company as the fund is focused on opportunity zones, leading to some tax-advantages to investors. The Fund has recently completed its two largest projects, Aster & Links and Viv, being at a pivotal moment to switch its investment case from a development phase into a recurring business. Despite that, it’s still trading at a significant discount to NAV, making it an interesting value play in the real estate sector. As the Fund has recently published its quarterly earnings related to Q3 2025, in this article I review its investment case and most recent financial performance, to see if it still offers value for long-term investors or not.

Recent Developments

Belpointe PREP has been developing some assets in recent years and has started leasing apartments in its largest project, Aster & Links located in Florida some months ago, while the Viv project was completed more recently and started its leasing activity last October. This means Belpointe PREP’s quarterly figures related to Q3 2025 do not yet include leasing activity related to Viv, which should start to generate rental income during Q4.

Due to the completion of these two projects, the Fund’s total assets increased to $570 million at the end of last September, representing an increase of 10% compared to the end of 2024. This increase is mainly justified by the increased value of its properties, namely related to Aster & Links and Viv. Indeed, at the end of Q3 2025, some 90% of its assets were related to real estate, with only $58 million being under construction compared to more than $190 million at the end of last year. Regarding its cash position, Belpointe PREP had more than $29 million of cash at the end of last September, compared to $24 million at the end of 2024, showing that the Fund has managed well its cash position and shows good liquidity as its two largest projects are now completed.

On the liabilities side, Belpointe PREP has refinanced its construction loan related to the Aster & Links asset after the end of Q3 2025, with an affiliate of Affinius Capital. Belpointe PREP entered a $204 million refinancing transaction last October, using the vast majority of proceeds to refinance existing debt and also used some cash to support the lease-up and stabilization of its Aster & Links asset. This refinancing is expected to save the Fund multiple million dollars on interest costs per year, providing a financial benefit for over the long term for the Fund and its investors. According to Belpointe PREP, more than 50% of the Aster & Links residential units are currently leased and has also a large retail offering supported by Sprouts Farmers Market but considering that the Fund started the leasing process one year ago this is not completely successful, as many apartments are still vacant.

Nevertheless, due to the leasing-up of rental properties in the Aster & Links asset, Belpointe PREP has started to generate meaningful rental income over the past few quarters, while previously the rental income was quite low. Indeed, during the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025), its revenues amounted to more than $6 million, compared to just $1.5 million during the same period of 2024, representing an increase of close to 300%. During the last quarter, its revenues were close to $2.4 million, which means its annualized revenues are now closer to $10 million, compared to its reported revenues of only $2.7 million in 2024.

This strong increase is explained by the Aster & Links relatively large size, as the asset owns some 424 apartments units and 51,000 square feet of ground-floor retail space. While there is still significant vacant space, this asset started to generate rental income in recent months and is expected to gradually lead to higher revenues over the next few months, boding well for Belpointe PREP’s financial figures ahead. Additionally, its Viv project was recently completed, and the Fund is now in the process of signing leases for the units, expecting to start generating rental income during the month of November.

This means that Belpointe PREP’s rental income should increase during Q4 2025 and most likely during the first quarters of 2026, if leasing momentum remains good in the short term especially in its Viv project that it is more recent. This asset is a development project with 269 residential units plus retail space in St. Petersburg, Florida, being smaller than Aster & Links, but it is still a sizable project that is expected to produce significant rental income and earnings over the next few years.

The completion of these projects shows that Belpointe PREP can execute on its business plan and is now in the early stages of starting to generate significant revenues and earnings, being an important shift in its investment case from a speculative development play to a ‘normal’ REIT that generates cash flow and, eventually, distributes dividends to investors. However, the Fund continues to have some development projects ongoing, which require investment and capital expenditures ahead, making dividends secondary for its investment case for the time being.

Moreover, from an operating perspective, its current quarterly revenues of $2.4 million in the last quarter were not enough to cover expenses of close to $12 million, which means the Fund is still in the red regarding its operating profitability. This means Belpointe PREP does not report accounting or tax profits, plus it is a qualified opportunity fund and not a REIT for tax purposes, which means it is not required to distribute 90% of its tax profit as dividends to unitholders as REITs are required to do.

Its net loss in Q3 2025 amounted to some $12 million and was above $28 million during the first nine months of 2025, being much higher than compared to the same periods of 2024, justified by higher operating expenses related to its properties.

From a cash flow perspective, Belpointe PREP’s operating cash flow was negative by close to $15 million during 9M 2025 and investment activities resulted in a cash outflow of $56 million during the same period, which were financed by taking new loans both for general corporate purposes and construction-related loans. The Fund wants to maintain leverage at reasonable levels, targeting a debt ratio between 50-70% over the long term. At the end of Q3 2025, Belpointe PREP’s total debt amounted to about $251 million, while its total assets were close to $571 million, which means its debt ratio was close to 44% being a small increase compared to 40% at the end of 2024.

This debt ratio is still below its target level, which means Belpointe PREP is likely to maintain its strategy of raising debt to finance its operating loss and negative cash flow generation ahead, potentially starting to generate cash flows from an operating basis over the next few quarters if the lease-up of both its Aster & Links and Viv projects remain on a positive path ahead.

Regarding other development projects, the Fund has identified other assets for future potential development and had more than $50 million of real estate valued under construction at the end of last quarter, but its Aster & Links and Viv projects were the largest ones and should be the main drivers of revenues and cash flow ahead. Going forward, its growth strategy is not expected to change much, focused on investing in the development of multifamily properties for rental income purposes in opportunity zones. Its business goal is to have a diversified investment portfolio that generates enough income to make regular distributions to unitholders in the future, something that should take some years to achieve as the Fund is still in an early-growth phase.

Valuation & Risks

Regarding its net asset value (NAV), Belpointe PREP has not updated this value since the end of 2024, when it was close to $120 per unit. As the market value of its properties has increased over the past few months, while net debt also was up by a similar amount during the same period, its NAV is likely to have remained more or less stable in recent quarters.

This means its valuation is likely to remain at a discounted value to its NAV given that Belpointe PREP is trading at 61$ per unit, compared to its last NAV reported of $120 per unit. Therefore, Belpointe PREP is currently trading at around 0.5x NAV, a lower valuation than when I last analyzed it, considering that it was trading at 0.55x NAV at the time. This makes Belpointe PREP an interesting value play for investors as the discount to NAV is quite large, but it’s also a reflection of its still high-risk profile as the Fund does not generate enough revenue and cash flows to make its business model sustainable over the long term.

Regarding major risks, I see its capacity to raise debt and refinance existing loans as key for the Fund’s business plan ahead, allowing it to keep investing in new developments as currently the operating activities don’t generate enough cash flow for the Fund to finance by itself its growth investments. Additionally, another major risk in my opinion is the leasing momentum, both at Aster & Links and Viv, which will show if there is market demand for its properties and if the Fund can execute on its business plan. If leasing momentum does not pick up ahead, this may put into question the Fund’s ability to pay a dividend in the future, something that is especially relevant in the real estate sector given that investors are usually attracted to this sector by its income appeal. If Belpointe PREP’s don’t improve its cash flow generation ahead, this could certainly be badly received by investors, and its unit price could probably continue to trade at a significant discount to NAV.

Conclusion

Belpointe PREP continues to trade at a significant discount to its last reported NAV and the business plan continues to be well executed, considering that more than half of its Aster & Links project is now leased and the Viv project is expected to start generating income soon. However, the Fund is still not reporting operating profits and transparency regarding the NAV could be better, as the Fund had not updated investors since the end of 2024, justifying to some extent a valuation discount. Having said that, I think trading at 0.5x NAV Belpointe PREP remains an interesting speculative play in the REIT sector, that can have significant upside potential if the Fund continues to execute well on its growth plan in the near future.
